coredata.dc:title: Superior photodynamic effect of carbon quantum dots through both type I and type II pathways: Detailed comparison study of top-down-synthesized and bottom-up-synthesized carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Pillar-Little, Timothy J.
Wanninayake, Namal
Nease, Leona
Heidary, David K.
Glazer, Edith C.
Kim, Doo Young
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been extensively studied for bioimaging and photodynamic applications due to their low cost, excellent biocompatibility, rich surface chemistry and controllable optical properties. However, the detailed mechanism of the photodynamic activity has been rarely reported. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of (i) a systematic comparison of differently synthesized CQDs to unveil a relationship between chemical structure and photodynamic effect, and (ii) their detailed mechanism of action in photodynamic effects. CQDs prepared by top-down and bottom-up methods and their post-synthesis modification were compared in this study. CQDs prepared by a top-down method exhibited superior light-activated cell cytotoxicity compared to those by a bottom-up approach. The photodynamic index of CQDs was found to be 40–150 times larger than commercial photodynamic agents. It was concluded that both structural defects in sp2-carbon domains and oxygen-containing chemical groups have a crucial role in the excellent photodynamic performance. Measurements with selective quenchers of 1O2 and radical species indicated that the photodynamic mechanism of CQDs is through the combination of both type I (radical species production) and type II (singlet oxygen production) pathways.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photodynamic mechanism of action: type I vs type II

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: TPL and DYK thank D. Qian for TEM characterizations. TPL and DYK appreciate the support from National Science Foundation under Cooperative Agreement No. 1355438 and the partial support from Kentucky Science & Engineering Foundation grant ( KSEF-3127-RDE-017 ). The purchase of a new XPS system recently installed at the University of Kentucky was supported by the fund from the NSF EPSCoR grant (grant no. 0814194 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of CQDs via two methods: A) Top-down synthesis of GQDs from carbon nano-onions. Post-synthesis modification of NGQDs by hydrothermally treating GQDs with aqueous ammonia or rGQDs by chemical reduction with NaBH4. B) Bottom-up synthesis of CNDs from citric acid. Post-synthesis modification yields NCNDs by hydrothermally treating CNDs with aqueous ammonia. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of CQDs. (Left) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) produced by a top-down method and (right) a bottom-up-synthesized carbon nanodots (CNDs). Inset: high-resolution TEM images of single dots. Size distribution curves in inset were constructed from TEM images including ∼80 carbon dots.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQD samples. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Photoluminescence spectra of GQD (top left), NGQD (bottom left), CND (top right), and NCND (bottom right). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Efficacy of compounds as PDT agents determined in HL60 cells. Cell viability determined for a) CND, b) NCND, c) GQD, and d) NGQD. Compounds were dosed with the cells and either exposed to indigo light (blue line) or kept in the dark (black line). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Efficiency of CQDs for the generation of singlet oxygen determined by the measurement of SOSG emission. The delta sign in Y axis indicates the difference in the emission signals of SOSG before and after its exposure to light. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Gel electrophoresis results of DNA damage experiment. It can be seen that there is a moderate amount of plasmid fragmentation when exposed to GQDs and light. Without either GQDs or light, there is no measurable fragmentation. “RB (+cntrl)” refers to a control solution of 100 μM Rose Bengal (without CQDs).
Efficacy of compounds as PDT agents determined in HL60 cells. Photodynamic index (ratio of dark cytotoxicity vs light cytotoxicity) is a measure of PDT efficacy.

head.ce:title: Superior photodynamic effect of carbon quantum dots through both type I and type II pathways: Detailed comparison study of top-down-synthesized and bottom-up-synthesized carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kentucky, Lexington, KY, 40506, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been extensively studied for bioimaging and photodynamic applications due to their low cost, excellent biocompatibility, rich surface chemistry and controllable optical properties. However, the detailed mechanism of the photodynamic activity has been rarely reported. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of (i) a systematic comparison of differently synthesized CQDs to unveil a relationship between chemical structure and photodynamic effect, and (ii) their detailed mechanism of action in photodynamic effects. CQDs prepared by top-down and bottom-up methods and their post-synthesis modification were compared in this study. CQDs prepared by a top-down method exhibited superior light-activated cell cytotoxicity compared to those by a bottom-up approach. The photodynamic index of CQDs was found to be 40–150 times larger than commercial photodynamic agents. It was concluded that both structural defects in sp2-carbon domains and oxygen-containing chemical groups have a crucial role in the excellent photodynamic performance. Measurements with selective quenchers of 1O2 and radical species indicated that the photodynamic mechanism of CQDs is through the combination of both type I (radical species production) and type II (singlet oxygen production) pathways.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a non-invasive therapeutic approach with several advantages including a low inherent cytotoxicity and local targeting through light-activated toxicity. A PDT agent is internalized into cells alone or through the aid of a carrier. Originally used as a treatment only for skin conditions such as vitiligo [1], it has recently been explored for actinic keratosis and basal cell carcinoma treatment [2]. One critical challenge to overcome in order to develop PDT as a viable treatment is to extend the penetration depth of light through tissue. UV and visible light is energetic enough to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), however UV light (λ < 400 nm) risks damaging healthy tissue. Visible light (400–760 nm) is safe for healthy tissues but has a short penetration depth because of the absorption of light by tissue [3]. In the case of visible light, photodynamic medical treatments are limited to cutaneous and subcutaneous applications. The range of light wavelengths suitable for therapeutic application is from 650 nm to 1350 nm, because of low light absorption by tissues as well as forward-directed scattering [4].

Small molecule photosensitizers are the most common types of PDT agents, but their absorption and emission maxima fall outside of the optical therapeutic window. As a potential alternative, carbon nanomaterials have been extensively studied due to their controllable optical properties by manipulating the optical bandgap generated from quantum confinement effect [5,6]. Despite promising optical properties toward the therapeutic window, near-IR emitting single-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) suffer from poor solubility in aqueous media [7]. Without the aid of surfactants or sidewall functionalization, these carbon nanotubes can aggregate into bundles which rupture cell membranes and induce unwanted cytotoxicity [8–13]. The cytotoxicity of CNTs without light-activation often exceed that of metal oxide nanoparticles [10–12,14,15]. This dark cytotoxicity casts some doubt on the use of CNTs as effective and non-toxic PDT agents.

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a promising deep-tissue PDT agent with controllable optical properties via chemical functionalization [16–19]. This new type of carbon-based nanoparticle has gained significant interest due to several attractive features such as widely tunable optical properties [20,21], ease of synthesis [22–25] and rich surface chemistry [26,27], resistance against photobleaching [28] as well as excellent biocompatibility [18,29]. Structurally, the carbon backbone of CQD is comprised of a flat sheet of sp2-hybridized nanographene that is edge-terminated with abundant functional groups (e.g. hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl). The lateral dimension of CQDs ranges from a few to 10 nm. CQDs can be synthesized either by re-assembling small molecules such as citric acid and glucose (bottom-up approach) [30,31] or breaking down larger precursors such as carbon black or graphene oxide (top-down approach) [32,33].

CQDs have shown efficacy as PDT agents in the areas of anti-bacterial and wound treatment despite suffering from low optical absorption and emission in the IR and near-IR range [19,34–36]. Long wavelength absorption and emission can be realized by controlling the morphology of a CQD with bottom-up synthesis as shown by Kumawat et al. or by removing internal defects in CQDs as reported by Fan et al. [37,38] In light of the extensive studies on CQDs exhibiting photodynamic effects towards inducing cytotoxicity in cancer cells and tumors, a fundamental understanding of what structural parameters allow CQDs to be useful PDT agents remains unclear. A recent report by Zhou et al. illustrated the role of oxygenated chemical groups on the catalytic generation of OH radicals, one common type of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [39].

In this work, we have compared the PDT efficacy of top-down-synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and bottom-up-synthesized carbon nanodots (CNDs) and their derivatives produced by either incorporating nitrogen dopants or hydrothermally being reduced. It was found that the aromatic core from a top-down synthesis along with oxygen-containing functional groups yielded the highest performing PDT agents. By incorporating nitrogen into top-down synthesized CQDs or chemically reducing these carbon nanomaterials, we observed the suppression of PDT activity. The bottom-up synthesis approach yielded a poorly performing PDT agent, which was enhanced by nitrogen-doping.

Overall, it was observed that both synthetic methods and post-synthesis modifications strongly impact the photodynamic performance. As-synthesized GQDs showed the photodynamic index 40 times larger than aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and 150 times larger than Foscan® [40,41]. Our results suggest that CQDs exhibit strong PDT activity in both the Type I (radical species production) and Type II (direct 1O2 production) processes. We find this unique result unifies previous works that have separately exhibited Type I and Type II photodynamic mechanisms of action [19,39]. Understanding the interplay between chemical structure and photodynamic mechanism of action will be essential to develop CQDs as non-toxic, low-cost, and highly effective PDT agents.
Details about structural characterizations of CQDs, cell viability tests, singlet oxygen measurements, and ROS measurements are included in supporting information.

Prior to the synthesis of GQDs, CNOs were prepared by thermal annealing of nanodiamond powders (Dynalene NB50) at 1650 °C for 1.0 h under the flow of Helium in a graphitization furnace. Then the top-down synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from CNOs was conducted as follows. CNOs (5 nm in diameter) in a 3:1 solution of concentrated HNO3 and H2SO4 were refluxed at 105 °C for 4 h. The resultant solution was neutralized with solid KOH pellet and filtered using a Buchner funnel (20 μm pore size) to remove salts and large particles. Typically, 50 mL of the filtered solution was placed into a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1 kDa) for 1 week to remove any remaining salts and then concentrated to 0.5 mg mL−1.

The bottom-up synthesis of carbon nanodots (CNDs) was done with citric acid as the carbon source. In a typical synthesis, 1 g of citric acid solid in 50 mL beaker was placed on a hotplate at 160 °C, and was pyrolyzed for 10 min. Then, 100 mL of 0.1 M KOH was added to quench the synthesis of CNDs and to suppress aggregation. The resultant solution was dialyzed for 1 week and concentrated to 0.5 mg mL−1.

Nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) were prepared by reacting 0.5 mg mL−1 GQDs with concentrated NH4OH (28 wt %) in a hydrothermal (HT) reactor. The HT reactor was placed in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h and then left to cool overnight. Afterwards, the resultant solution was gently heated to remove excess NH3 and then dialyzed for 1 week to remove excess ions. Chemically-reduced GQDs (rGQDs) were prepared by treating with NaBH4. Typically, 10 mg of GQD in DI water was treated with 200 mg of NaBH4 for 1 h while the solution was stirred at room temperature. Afterwards, the solution was neutralized with dilute H2SO4, then dialyzed for 1 week using 1 kDa MWCO dialysis bag. Nitrogen-doped CNDs (NCNDs) were prepared in the same manner as NGQDs. 5 mL of 0.5 mg mL−1 CNDs were mixed with 5 mL of concentrated NH4OH (28 wt %) and placed in a hydrothermal (HT) reactor with no additional gas pressure applied. The HT reactor was placed in an oven at 180 °C for 5 h and left to cool overnight. The resulting solution was gently heated to remove excess NH3 and then dialyzed for 1 week to remove excess ions.

To determine the relationship between chemical structure of CQDs and photodynamic effect, carbon dots were synthesized via both top-down and bottom-up methods. The overall synthetic schemes for CQDs are in Fig. 1 . As shown in Fig. 1a–b, top-down-synthesized carbon dots will be denoted as “graphene quantum dots” (GQDs). GQDs are similar to ones synthesized by chemical oxidation [32,38,42] and electrochemical exfoliation [43–45]. The bottom-up approach produced carbon dots termed “carbon nanodots” (CNDs) as described in previous studies with microwave pyrolysis [31,46,47] and hydrothermal treatment [48,49]. The detailed procedures of CQD synthesis and subsequent modification are described in the experimental section (2.1 and 2.2).

Fig. 2 shows the results from transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiments. These micrographs illustrate that the undoped CQDs have an average diameter of 4.8 nm for GQDs and 8.8 nm for CNDs. Furthermore, these diameters are not affected by the sample modification procedures detailed in Section 2.2. In high-resolution TEM, lattice fringes of ∼0.2 nm in size were observed for GQDs and CNDs, which corresponds to (1120) plane of a graphene sheet, indicating that the carbon dots include sp2-hybridized carbon domains. The presence of these sp2-hybridized carbon domains were confirmed by both Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

For a detailed analysis of the chemical structure, samples were characterized with XPS and FTIR to probe the specific distribution of chemical states in each CQD. High resolution XPS C1s and N1s spectra for all CQD samples are found in the Supporting Information (Figs. S1, S2, and S3). Tables S1 and S2 show the summary of XPS analyses, including the atomic percentages of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements as well as the contents of sp2 vs sp3 carbon. These XPS spectra show that both CND and GQD have similar overall oxygen contents but with varying distributions of functional groups (i.e.: hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl). The peaks are deconvoluted into CO hydroxyl (286.0 eV), carbonyl groups (287.6 eV) associated with aldehydes and ketones, and carboxylic groups (288.6 eV). In the bottom panels of Fig. S1, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NGQDs and NCNDs) have C1s XPS spectra similar to their undoped counterparts but with notable changes in sp3-carbon content. After deconvolution, the C1s XPS spectra of NGQDs and NCNDs revealed the emergence of additional subpeaks, which can be attributed to amine, amide and imine N groups. In Fig. S3, the N1s XPS spectra of NGQD and NCNDs are deconvoluted into pyridinic (398.7 eV), amine/amide N (399.6 eV), pyrrolic (400.5 eV), quaternary N (401.6 eV), and N-oxide (402.7 eV). Interestingly, substantial quantity of quaternary N (401.6 eV) is present in NGQD while it is missing in NCND. Amine/amide N states are formed by reaction of NH4OH with abundant defects and furthermore transformed to pyridinic, pyrrolic and quaternary N states through hydrothermal treatment.

FT-IR spectra of CQDs (Figs. S4 and S5) confirmed the previous XPS interpretations by revealing peaks assigned to hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and aromatic carbon. In Fig. S4 (left), after hydrothermal reaction of GQDs with aqueous ammonia, NGQDs show new peaks indicative of a successful reaction. Conversely, the FT-IR spectra of CNDs (Fig. S4, right) show similar oxygen-containing functional groups like GQDs but with intensities that correspond to the varied functional group distribution. Unique to CNDs, there is a significant sp3-carbon peak (CH stretch) that is removed upon hydrothermal treatment. Like GQDs, the same shift of OH to NH2 occurs when ammonia is added to CNDs. The simultaneous shift from the 1200 and 3400 cm−1 peaks to 1050 cm−1 and 3300 cm−1 is attributed to the replacement of hydroxyl groups (CO and OH stretches) with N-containing functional groups (CN and NH stretches), respectively. Carboxylic acid groups are converted to amide groups, signified by the shift of the peak from 1750 cm−1 to 1650 cm-1 and the loss of the well-defined peak at 2500 cm−1. In Fig. S5, rGQD shows a small suppression of peaks relating to carbonyl groups at 1750 cm−1. This is coupled with the increase of peak intensity at 1100 cm−1 corresponding to hydroxyl groups. The conversion of carbonyl groups to hydroxyl groups by chemical reduction revealed in FT-IR (Fig. S5) is consistent with XPS spectra in Figs. S1 and S2.

Fig. 3 shows the UV-VIS absorption spectra of 4 different carbon dots. In general, compared with GQDs (pristine and nitrogen-doped GQDs), CNDs (pristine and nitrogen doped CNDs) show blue-shifted absorption spectra. CNDs tend to absorb and emit at shorter wavelengths due to smaller sp2 nanodomains that are well passivated which block non-radiative relaxation. This is also the case in Fig. 3 where CQD samples of the same concentration (0.05 mg mL−1) were analyzed by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. Upon nitrogenation, both CND and GQD samples increase their absorbance at all wavelengths. However, it is important to note that NGQDs absorb a wider range of visible light than all the other CQD samples.

Overall, the photoluminescence (PL) of CQDs in this report exhibit optical properties similar to the report by Wang et al. that details intrinsic emission from the aromatic carbon core and extrinsic emission originating from molecule-like fluorophores [50]. In Fig. 4 , both GQDs and NGQDs have excitation-dependent emission profiles. GQDs show 520 nm peak emission at 450 nm excitation and NGQDs show peak 520 nm emission with 420 nm excitation. Both of these are attributed to extrinsic emissions. Chemical reduction of GQD resulted in peak emission wavelengths that were shifted to shorter wavelengths (Supporting Information, Fig. S6). In comparison with GQDs, CNDs display blue-shifted photoluminescence with 450 nm peak emission upon to excitation at 330 nm. We attribute the blue-shift of emission to surface-passivated aromatic domains formed during synthesis [51]. Compared to CNDs, NCNDs were blue shifted by 30 nm with the same excitation wavelength.

Photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of CQDs were also determined. In general, with a strong radiative decay process competing with PDT pathways, the quantum yield for type I and type II PDT processes is reduced. Table S3 shows the absolute PLQY of each CQD sample as determined by an integrating sphere. Overall, nitrogenation and chemical reduction increase the PLQY of both CNDs and GQDs. Enhancement by the addition of nitrogen has been extensively studied for its effect on optical properties [17,20]. Reduction of carbonyl groups to hydroxyls which has also been shown to increase photoluminescent QY [52–55]. CNDs inherently have a higher PLQY than GQDs due to the effect of surface passivation around aromatic domains [51]. NCNDs show a significant increase of PLQY compared to CNDs which is consistent with many reports that dope nitrogen into carbon quantum dots. Based on high-resolution XPS analysis in Fig. S1, we assign the PLQY enhancement in NCNDs to the increase of dipole moments associated with nitrogen-related chemical states.

The efficacy of CQDs as PDT agents was evaluated in a leukemic cell line (HL60). The cells were dosed with CQDs and irradiated for 1 min with 450 nm light or kept in the dark. Viability was measured after 72 h (Fig. 5 and S7). In each graph of Fig. 5 and S7, the leftmost point represents a control experiment in which cells are not dosed with CQDs (CQD concentration = 0 mg mL−1). As hypothesized, the photodynamic efficacy of CNDs and GQDs was largely dependent on chemical and surface structures. As summarized in Table 1 , GQDs were the most effective PDT agent among all the CQD samples tested. In HL60 cells, GQD and NGQD concentrations up to 0.1 mg mL−1 were not cytotoxic as shown in Fig. 5 c and Fig. 5d. When GQDs were exposed to 450 nm light, cytotoxicity occurred with an EC50 value of 10−3 mg mL−1. These results give a GQD photodynamic index of nearly 725, which is 40 times more potent than commercial PDT drugs such as aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and 150 times more potent than Foscan® [40,41]. Comparing EC50 values reported in recent articles [56–58], we found that our unmodified GQDs show strong light-induced cytotoxic effects with lower doses. NGQDs and rGQDs showed impressive photodynamic indices, 510 and 200, respectively, which greatly exceed the performance of ALA and Foscan® (Table 1).

Unmodified CNDs demonstrate no PDT activity in HL60 cells. Cytotoxicity occurred at concentrations of over 0.1 mg mL−1 in the absence or presence of irradiation. However, N-doped CNDs “switch on” a much stronger photodynamic effect toward cancer cells. We hypothesize that the new nitrogen-containing functional groups allow for facile electron transfer allowing the generation of ROS (OH· or OOH·).

After the cell viability experiments, we considered that a physical mechanism such as cell lysis (or contact-kill mechanism) could be partially responsible for those results. Fig. S8 illustrates that covalently anchored CQDs show no cytotoxicity, indicating the photodynamic activities of CQDs are greatly influenced by whether CQDs are bound to a substrate or free.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodynamic mechanism of action: type I vs type II
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, mechanistic studies were conducted for the observed PDT activity. Fig. 6 shows the amount of (1O2) singlet oxygen generated by different types of CQDs. A fluorescent SOSG dye was used as a measure of singlet oxygen. Consistent with negligible, light-induced toxicity shown in Fig. 5, CNDs produced very little singlet oxygen and only with very high concentrations did the NCNDs show measurable production of singlet oxygen. Compared to CNDs, GQDs showed pronounced performance for 1O2 generation. Both rGQDs and NGQDs were less efficient in the generation of 1O2 than the pristine GQDs, but greatly exceeding CNDs. The performance of each type of CQDs for the 1O2 generation is in the great agreement with the cytotoxicity results shown in Fig. 5.

These results strongly imply that both structural defects in sp2-carbon domains and the presence of oxygenated functional groups are crucial for the efficient generation of singlet oxygen. When the pristine aromatic core of GQD is disrupted by graphitic nitrogen, photodynamic activity is suppressed. In addition, oxygen-containing functional groups such as carbonyl groups seems to be essential for photodynamic activity. It is because chemical reduction with NaBH4 or hydrothermal treatment with aqueous ammonia, there is a severe decrease in singlet oxygen production. These findings are consistent with the recent article by Zhou et al. in which carbonyl groups were found to be catalytic sites for ROS production [39]. Our present work demonstrates that top-down synthesized GQDs, NGQDs, rGQDs outperform bottom-up synthesized CNDs and NCNDs, implying that structural defects in sp2-carbon domains and oxygenated chemical groups are crucial for efficient production of reactive oxygen species.

Damage of a DNA plasmid was also investigated to probe Type I and Type II mechanisms of action. Compounds were incubated with plasmid and illuminated in the presence or absence of ROS selective quenchers. As ROS is generated, the supercoiled plasmid sustains either single or double strand breaks, resulting in relaxed circle or linear plasmid. The amount of these plasmid species was quantified for GQDs, NGQDs and rGQDs (Fig. 7 ). The results for the other CQDs can be found in the Supporting Information (Fig. S9).

As expected, GQD-plasmid solution without light irradiation clearly shows very little damage to the plasmid DNA. The irradiated GQD-plasmid and NGQD-plasmid sample shows significant damage – even more so than 100 μM Rose Bengal, well-known photodynamic sensitizer. DNA damage was measured in the presence of several selective quenching molecules. Sodium azide (NaN3) was used to selectively quench singlet oxygen (1O2). TEMPO, a common radical scavenger, was used to trap radical ROS and sodium iodide (NaI) as a hydroxyl radical quenching agent. DNA damage was significantly reduced for both GQDs, NGQDs and rGQDs when the solutions were irradiated in the presence of NaN3, TEMPO, or NaI.

These results indicate that singlet oxygen is not the only ROS species produced by GQDs and their modified counterparts. In fact, there is little preference for a specific type of ROS and the mixture of superoxide, hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radical, and other radical species produced play a substantial part in the photodynamic death of cancer cells. In the case of NCNDs (Fig. S9) which showed strong light-activated cytotoxicity, the photodynamic mechanism of action is largely driven by radical ROS.

In Fig. 7 70% DNA damage was found for unmodified GQDs and 45% DNA damage for reduced GQDs, clearly implying that oxygen functional groups in CQDs are critical for efficient photodynamic actions. Another important finding in Fig. 7 is that the overall mechanism of action for CQDs involves the combination of both Type 1 and Type II processes. This is evidenced by the fact that the greatest quenching in % DNA damage is observed with either NaN3 (singlet oxygen scavenger) or TEMPO (radical scavenger). Our observations are in good agreements with the reports by Alegría et al. and Crisostomo et al. that photochemistry of quinones and their derivatives is responsible for the production of singlet oxygen and superoxides with high singlet oxygen quantum yields [59,60]. These reports include 1,4-napthaquinones and sulfonate-substituted anthraquinones (for water solubility) which can be good small molecule analogues for the edge-terminating carbonyls found in CQDs.

NGQDs are particularly interesting as they produce intermediate results between GQDs and rGQDs in cell viability, 1O2 production, and DNA damage experiments. We have observed through XPS characterization that the conversion of oxygen-containing functional groups diverges into several nitrogen-containing functional groups such as pyridinic, amine, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N. The introduction of these functional groups coincides with a loss in singlet oxygen production.

For nitrogen-doped CNDs, the mechanism of action shifts towards a Type I process due to a very low singlet oxygen production and total suppression of DNA damage when TEMPO is the quenching agent. There is a correlation from our FT-IR and XPS characterizations with the DNA damage studies that suggests N-sites generate radical species. We wish to pursue further studies nitrogen-doped CNDs that would concretely demonstrate if some or all of these N-sites do generate radical species exclusively.

We cannot determine whether the PDT activity of these functional groups, or molecule-like states, comes from direct energy transfer to molecular oxygen from the excited triplet state of the functional group, or via indirect energy transfer between the intrinsic state and the molecule-like state. Nonetheless, the trends found across GQDs, NGQDs, and rGQDs in this study suggest that structural defects in aromatic domains and oxygen-containing functional groups play an important role toward the Type I and Type II mechanisms of action. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report that (i) compares the photodynamic activity of CQDs prepared by different synthetic approaches and (ii) reveals the involvement of both Type I and Type II mechanistic pathways.

Herein we report the first study to compare differently synthesized CQDs to unveil relations between chemical structure of CQDs and photodynamic effects. CQDs synthesized via top-down and bottom-up methods were characterized and compared. GQDs and their chemically modified CQDs demonstrated excellent PDT activity toward HL60 cancer cell lines using 450 nm light as the excitation source. Nearly all CQD samples show a significant enhancement of photodynamic index in comparison to ALA and Foscan® with unmodified GQDs showing the largest increase and unmodified CNDs showing little to no photo-induced cytotoxicity. The careful comparison of CQD samples produced by top-down and bottom-up methods reflect that the pronounced PDT effects comes from the combination of structural defects in sp2-carbon domains and oxygen-containing chemical groups. Through singlet oxygen measurements and DNA damage experiments performed with gel electrophoresis, the fine mechanism of action in each of the CQD samples was unveiled. For the first time, both Type I and Type II photodynamic processes were observed to be involved in the production of ROS by the photoactivation of GQDs, NGQDs, and rGQDs. The present study provides not only valuable insight on the structure-activity relation of photodynamic CQDs, but also a viable route to engineer the surface chemistry of CQDs for the advanced photodynamic therapeutics of CQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: TPL and DYK thank D. Qian for TEM characterizations. TPL and DYK appreciate the support from National Science Foundation under Cooperative Agreement No. 1355438 and the partial support from Kentucky Science & Engineering Foundation grant (KSEF-3127-RDE-017). The purchase of a new XPS system recently installed at the University of Kentucky was supported by the fund from the NSF EPSCoR grant (grant no. 0814194).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.09.004.
